case_metadata.court: Armed Services Board of Contract Appeals
case_metadata.project_type: HVAC system design and construction for healthcare clinic

nodes.description: Government directed JE Dunn to redesign boilers with 15% safety factor and humidifiers for -30°F winter design temperature, effectively upsizing both systems.
nodes.source_excerpt: Government rejected boiler submittal on March 10, 2020 and humidifier submittal on March 30, 2020, requiring compliance with contract specifications.

nodes.description: Government repeatedly rejected hydronic piping shop drawings with E-codes for various deficiencies including insulation issues and safety factor compliance.
nodes.source_excerpt: Multiple rejections of hydronic shop drawings between April and June 2020 for insulation, safety factor, and other technical requirements.

nodes.description: Mold discovered in ductwork requiring remediation work that overtook the project's critical path.
nodes.source_excerpt: Mold discovered in ductwork on May 18, 2020, remained on critical path until April 2022.

nodes.description: Government halted hydronic piping installation pending design corrections and required JE Dunn to submit design correction narrative.
nodes.source_excerpt: ACO directed JE Dunn to provide revised design meeting contract requirements before proceeding.

nodes.description: JE Dunn's delay analysis expert acknowledged possible concurrent delays overlapping with alleged government-caused delays, requiring forensic analysis to identify but none was performed.
nodes.source_excerpt: Ms. Meeks conceded possible concurrent project delay overlapping alleged delays that her analysis does not identify

nodes.description: Mold remediation overtook the project's critical path on June 19, 2020 and remained critical until April 2022, but was excluded from JE Dunn's delay analysis.
nodes.source_excerpt: Mold remediation overtook critical path on June 19, 2020 and was still critical in July 2020

nodes.description: Government rejected hydronic shop drawing submittals for insulation issues (strainers, valves) and other deficiencies starting April 17, 2020, which were not included in delay analysis.
nodes.source_excerpt: April 17, 2020 rejection of Transmittal No. 23 64 26-5 regarding piping insulation for strainers and valves

nodes.description: JE Dunn submitted a claim to the contracting officer on July 19, 2021 demanding $949,054 and a 57-day time extension for government-directed changes to humidifiers and boilers and rejection of hydronic piping submittals.
nodes.source_excerpt: JE Dunn presented to the contracting officer a claim demanding $949,054 and a time extension of 57 compensable calendar days

nodes.description: Government refused to approve any construction submittals until JE Dunn revised humidifier and boiler designs, preventing progress on hydronic piping installation despite no reasonable relationship between the systems.
nodes.source_excerpt: Government refused to allow JE Dunn to proceed with installation of hydronic piping until humidifier and boiler designs were revised

nodes.description: Government rejected JE Dunn's hydronic shop drawings submittal on May 13, 2020 due to issues including perimeter heating system, space heating, and insulation, contributing to the halt of hydronic piping installation.
nodes.source_excerpt: Government rejected hydronic shop drawings on May 13, 2020 for issues including perimeter heating system, space heating, and insulation

nodes.description: Government lifted the hold on hydronic piping installation via July 6, 2020 letter from ACO, allowing work to proceed after previous rejections.
nodes.source_excerpt: ACO told JE Dunn on July 6, 2020 that hydronic piping installation could proceed

nodes.description: Government approved hydronic shop drawings on July 10, 2020, ending the submittal rejection cycle for this component.
nodes.source_excerpt: Government approved the hydronic shop drawings on July 10, 2020

nodes.description: Government delayed hydronic piping installation by linking it to separate boiler and humidifier design issues despite the components being independent, preventing JE Dunn from proceeding with installation.
nodes.source_excerpt: Government admitted that boiler/humidifier issues were separate from hydronic piping but still linked approval, delaying critical path activity

edges.source_citation: Hydronic rejection references to safety factor and design requirements
Sequence of rejections leading to ACO's design freeze directive
text discussion of sequential submittal rejections
context of multiple submittal rejections prolonging design freeze
July 6, 2020 letter ending hold on installation

extraction_notes: Relationship between mold discovery and other delays not clearly established in this part. Fin tube radiation equipment rejection mentioned but unclear how it relates to hydronic piping delays.
Mold remediation event (n6) appears to be the same event as n3 from previous part, but with additional critical path impact details. Maintained as separate node due to new critical path context.
The 57-day delay period referenced appears inconsistent with earlier 77/84-day claims; exact duration and critical path impact remain contested

The May 13, 2020 rejection was mentioned in hearing testimony but not in post-hearing briefing, creating some ambiguity about its formal inclusion in the case. The relationship between fin tube radiation equipment installation and hydronic piping installation remains unexplained in the text.
Court acknowledges government-caused delay but rejects JE Dunn's specific delay period quantification (57 days) due to insufficient evidence of start/end dates on critical path
